critique.summary: The article describes the author's process of building a simple Unix shell called 'andsh' in C, explaining key shell features such as command execution, environment variable expansion, piping, and basic line editing with history and tab completion. It provides code snippets and explanations of how these features are implemented at a low level using Unix system calls.
mainPoints: The author builds a toy shell named 'andsh' that supports running commands, changing directories, environment variable expansion, and basic piping.
The shell uses a read-eval-print loop (REPL) to interactively read commands, tokenize input, expand environment variables, and execute commands using fork and execvp.
Built-in commands like 'cd' must run in the shell process to affect the shell's state.
Pipes are implemented using the pipe() system call and dup2 to connect stdout and stdin between processes in a pipeline.
Line editing, command history, and tab completion are added using the readline library.
The shell is intentionally minimal and incomplete, lacking features like quoting, redirection, and advanced builtins.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the implementation of a basic Unix shell in C, correctly explaining the use of system calls like fork, execvp, pipe, dup2, and waitpid for process management and interprocess communication. The explanations of shell concepts such as built-in commands, environment variable expansion, and piping align with standard Unix shell behavior. The use of the readline library for line editing and history is also factual. The article does not make unsupported claims and provides code examples consistent with common shell implementation techniques.
considerations: The article does not address security implications of shell implementation, such as input sanitization or handling malicious commands.
It omits discussion of more advanced shell features like quoting, redirection, job control, and signal handling beyond basic interrupts.
Performance considerations of the readline-based completion and the potential overhead of system calls per prompt are only briefly mentioned.
The shell's limitations in handling complex command syntax and scripting capabilities are acknowledged but not deeply explored.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the challenges and learning experiences involved in building a Unix shell, particularly focusing on string parsing, pipe implementation, and job control. Several commenters share personal experiences, useful resources, and technical insights about the complexity hidden beneath the shell's simplicity.

comments.author: wei03288
comments.text: The pipe section is the part that changes how you think about processes. Once you've manually done the dup2 dance — close write-end in parent, close read-end in child, wire them up — it stops being magic and starts being obvious why `grep | sort | uniq` works at all. The thing that surprised me building a similar toy was how late in the process job control has to come: you can get a working pipe chain surprisingly fast, and then job control (SIGTSTP, tcsetpgrp, the whole mess) costs 5x more than everything else combined.
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed technical insight into pipe implementation and job control, reflecting a deep understanding of Unix process management.

comments.author: rigorclaw
comments.text: The pipe implementation section is really clean. Working through fork/exec/dup2 by hand like this is one of those exercises that makes you appreciate how much composability Unix got right. Processes that know nothing about each other just work together because they read stdin and write stdout. I built something similar years ago and the moment pipes actually worked felt like unlocking a cheat code.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately praises the pipe implementation and highlights the educational value of manually handling process communication.

comments.author: hexer303
comments.text: Unix shells are conceptually simple but hide a surprising amount of complexity under the hood that we take for granted. I recently had build my own PTY controller. There were so many edge-cases to deal with. It took weeks of stress testing and writing many tests to get it right.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly points out the hidden complexity in shell components like PTY controllers, emphasizing the effort required to handle edge cases.

comments.author: lvales
comments.text:
Building a shell is a great exercise, but honestly having to deal with string parsing is such a bother that it robs like 2/3 of the joy along the way. I once built a very simple one in Go [0] as a learning exercise and I stopped once I started getting frustrated with all the corner cases.

[0] https://github.com/lourencovales/codecrafters/blob/master/sh...
comments.analysis: This comment provides a realistic perspective on the difficulties of string parsing in shell building, which is a common pain point.

comments.author: ratzkewatzke
comments.text: There's a very good exercise on Codecrafters (https://app.codecrafters.io/courses/shell/overview) to walk you through writing your own shell. I found it enlightening, as well as a good way to learn a new language.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a helpful resource for learning shell implementation, which is relevant and useful but less technically detailed.

comments.author: emersion
comments.text: Some time ago I've written an article about a particular aspect of shells, job control: https://emersion.fr/blog/2019/job-control/
comments.analysis: This comment shares an informative resource on job control, contributing useful background information.

comments.author: mzs
comments.text:
Had an assignment to build a shell in a week, how hard could it be?

  controlling terminal
  session leader
  job control
The parser was easy in comparison.
comments.analysis: The comment reflects a common experience that job control and terminal management are more challenging than parsing.

comments.author: zokier
comments.text: Bit of pedantry but I don't think traditional unix shell (like this) follows repl model; the shell is not usually doing printing of the result of evaluation. Instead the printing happens more as a side effect of the commands.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly clarifies a conceptual distinction about shell behavior versus a REPL, showing attention to detail.

comments.author: dirk94018
comments.text: Interesting. I wanted to do toast | bash to let the AI drive the computer but the bash shell really got in the way. Too much complexity. The things that annoy humans, $ expansion, special characters, etc don't work for AI either. Ended up writing a custom shell for AI (and humans). When a tool gets in the way, sometimes it just time to change the tool.
comments.analysis: The comment shares an innovative use case and highlights limitations of traditional shells for AI interaction.

comments.author: lioeters
comments.text: Link was previously posted by author: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47398749 There are other good quality articles on their site, and maybe deserves the imaginary points.
comments.analysis: This comment provides meta information about the link and endorses the quality of related articles, but adds little technical insight.

comments.author: austy69
comments.text: Fun read. Wonder if you are able to edit text in the shell, or if you need to implement a gap buffer to allow it?
comments.analysis: The comment poses a relevant question about shell input editing but does not provide further information or analysis.
